title: Core Java Concurrency Consolidated Workshop Quiz
answerConvention: Correct answer is stored first in options and answer is 0; the HTML deterministically shuffles display order.

questions.question: What does atomicity mean in this guide?
options: An operation cannot be observed partially complete
A thread always runs on one CPU core
questions.explanation: Atomicity is about all-or-nothing execution of an operation.

questions.question: What is visibility about?
options: Whether one thread is guaranteed to see another thread's write
Whether a variable has a short name
Whether logs are printed to stdout
questions.explanation: Visibility is the condition under which writes from one thread become observable by another.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a race condition?
options: Behavior depends on the ordering of actions across threads
The program has too many classes
Only one thread is ever created
questions.explanation: A race condition is about an unsafe interleaving of operations.

questions.question: Which situation is closest to a data race?
options: Two threads access the same non-final variable without proper synchronization and one writes
Two threads read a final constant
One thread creates an object and keeps it local
A program uses a queue with one producer
questions.explanation: Data races involve conflicting unsynchronized access to shared non-final state.

questions.question: Why is happens-before useful?
options: It tells you when one thread's effects must be visible to another
It makes all code run single-threaded
questions.explanation: Happens-before is the Java Memory Model relation used to reason about visibility and ordering.

questions.question: What does synchronized provide when used correctly?
options: Mutual exclusion and visibility through the same monitor
questions.explanation: synchronized protects code with a monitor and creates visibility between release and later acquisition of that monitor.

questions.question: What monitor does a non-static synchronized method use?
questions.explanation: A non-static synchronized method locks the receiver object.

questions.question: What does it mean that intrinsic locks are reentrant?
options: A thread that already owns the monitor can acquire it again
The lock is stored in ThreadLocal
questions.explanation: Reentrancy allows nested acquisition by the same thread.

questions.question: What must be true before calling wait on an object?
options: The current thread must hold that object's monitor
The object must be a Thread
questions.explanation: wait/notify/notifyAll require ownership of the target object's monitor.

questions.question: Why should wait be used inside a loop?
options: To re-check the condition after timing races or spurious wakeups
To make the loop consume CPU while blocked
questions.explanation: The loop waits for the condition, not just for a notification event.

modules.name: Volatile, atomics, ThreadLocal, and safe publication

questions.question: What guarantee does a volatile write provide for a later read of the same variable?
options: The write happens-before the later read
The operation becomes a transaction across all fields
The JVM creates a new thread
questions.explanation: Volatile creates a happens-before relationship from write to later read of the same volatile variable.

questions.question: Which use case fits volatile best?
options: A check-then-act lazy initialization across several fields
questions.explanation: Volatile is best for simple one-variable visibility protocols.

questions.question: What is compareAndSet commonly used for?
questions.explanation: compareAndSet lets one thread update a value only if it still has the expected value.

questions.question: What problem does ThreadLocal mainly avoid?
questions.explanation: ThreadLocal gives each thread its own value, reducing shared-state locking needs.

questions.question: Which action is unsafe during construction?
options: Allowing this to escape before the object is fully built
questions.explanation: If this escapes during construction, another thread may observe incomplete state.

questions.question: Why are immutable objects useful in concurrent programs?
options: Their observable state cannot change after construction
They require synchronized on every getter
They cannot be stored in collections
questions.explanation: Immutable objects are thread-safe when they are safely constructed and published.

modules.name: Threads, interruption, exception handling, and liveness

questions.question: What does BLOCKED usually mean for a Java thread?
options: It is waiting to acquire a monitor
questions.explanation: BLOCKED is the state for waiting to enter a monitor-protected section.

questions.question: What does Thread.join do?
options: Waits until the target thread finishes
questions.explanation: join blocks until the joined thread terminates.

questions.question: What should code often do if it catches InterruptedException but cannot throw it?
options: Restore the interrupted status before throwing an appropriate exception
questions.explanation: Restoring the interrupt flag gives higher-level code a chance to notice cancellation.

questions.question: What is the classic cause of deadlock in the account-transfer pattern?
options: Different threads acquire the same locks in opposite orders
The code uses too few comments
questions.explanation: Opposite lock acquisition order can create a waiting cycle.

questions.question: Which technique directly helps avoid deadlock with multiple locks?
questions.explanation: A consistent lock order prevents cycles in lock acquisition.

questions.question: How does livelock differ from deadlock?
options: Threads keep reacting but still make no useful progress
The JVM refuses to load classes
questions.explanation: Livelock has activity without progress.

questions.question: What is the main role of ExecutorService?
options: Submitting and managing task execution without manually creating a Thread per task
questions.explanation: ExecutorService is the central task-execution abstraction in java.util.concurrent.

questions.question: Which task type returns a value?
questions.explanation: Callable represents a computation with a return value.

questions.question: What does Future.get commonly risk if used without a timeout?
questions.explanation: Future.get waits for the result and can block.

questions.question: Why use ReentrantLock instead of synchronized?
options: For capabilities such as tryLock or interruptible lock acquisition
Because it turns lists into maps
questions.explanation: ReentrantLock provides features not available through intrinsic synchronization.

questions.question: What is CountDownLatch best suited for?
options: A one-time gate that opens after a count reaches zero
A resettable cyclic barrier in this guide
questions.explanation: CountDownLatch is initialized with a count and is not reusable after it reaches zero.

questions.question: What is a CompletableFuture advantage over plain Future?
options: It can compose async callbacks and pipelines without only blocking for results
It makes all callbacks run in the main thread
It removes the need for executors forever
questions.explanation: CompletableFuture supports chained stages and completion callbacks.

questions.question: When is CopyOnWriteArrayList usually a good fit?
questions.explanation: Copy-on-write makes writes expensive but reads and iteration cheap.

questions.question: Which collection is the general high-throughput concurrent map choice?
questions.explanation: ConcurrentHashMap is designed for concurrent key/value access.

questions.question: Which map/set family is useful when sorted concurrent access matters?
questions.explanation: Skip-list collections provide sorted concurrent structures.

questions.question: What does BlockingQueue add to Queue?
options: Methods that handle full or empty cases by blocking or timing out
A way to stop all threads
questions.explanation: BlockingQueue supports producer-consumer waiting behavior.

questions.question: Which queue performs direct handoff with no queue capacity?
questions.explanation: SynchronousQueue has zero capacity and pairs producer with consumer.

questions.question: What should guide collection choice?
options: Access pattern, ordering needs, blocking behavior, and contention
Whether it appears first in documentation
questions.explanation: Concurrent collection choice depends on workload and semantics.
